title: When "idle" isn't idle: how a Linux kernel optimization became a QUIC bug
summary: CUBIC, standardized in RFC 9438 , is the default congestion controller in Linux, and as a result governs how most TCP and QUIC connections on the public Internet probe for available bandwidth, back off when they detect loss, and recover afterward. At Cloudflare, our open-source implementation of QUIC, quiche , uses CUBIC as its default congestion controller, meaning this code is in the critical...
timeline_entries: CUBIC, standardized in RFC 9438 , is the default congestion controller in Linux, and as a result governs how most TCP and QUIC connections on the public Internet probe for available bandwidth, back off when they detect l
In this post, we’ll tell the story of a bug in which CUBIC's congestion window (cwnd) gets permanently pinned at its minimum and never recovers from a congestion collapse event.

The central knob a CCA turns is the congestion window ( cwnd ): the sender-side cap on how many bytes can be in flight (sent but not yet acknowledged) at any moment. A larger cwnd lets the sender push more data per round
The symptom: a test that fails 61% of the time

Our investigation started with the report of unexpected failures in our ingress proxy integration test pipeline. This erratic behavior appeared in tests where CUBIC was evaluated in a scenario of heavy loss in the early

Recovery after congestion collapse is an uncommon regime, but it is exactly the regime a congestion controller exists to handle. Most congestion control tests exercise the steady-state and growth phases of an algorithm;

sections.overview: CUBIC, standardized in RFC 9438 , is the default congestion controller in Linux, and as a result governs how most TCP and QUIC connections on the public Internet probe for available bandwidth, back off when they detect loss, and recover afterward. At Cloudflare, our open-source implementation of QUIC, quiche , uses CUBIC as its default congestion controller, meaning this code is in the critical path for a significant share of the traffic we serve. In this post, we’ll tell the story of a bug in which CUBIC's congestion window (cwnd) gets permanently pinned at its minimum and never recovers from a congestion collapse event.
sections.technical_breakdown:
The story starts with a Linux kernel change aimed at bringing CUBIC into line with the app-limited exclusion described in RFC 9438 §4.2-12 — a fix to a real problem in TCP that, when ported to our QUIC implementation, surfaced unexpected behaviors in quiche.

The family of loss-based algorithms, to which CUBIC belongs, operate on a fundamental premise: (1) if there is no packet loss, increase the sending rate (i.e. increase the bandwidth utilization); (2) if there is loss, loss-based algorithms assume that the network's capacity has been exceeded, and the sender must back off (i.e. decrease the bandwidth utilization).

Bugs in this corner of the state space are invisible in throughput dashboards, undetectable by static review, and only surface when you deliberately drive a CCA into it and watch whether it can climb back out — which is exactly what this test did.

The simulated test setup includes the following details:

 
 
 Quiche HTTP/3 client and server running at locally (localhost)

 RTT = 10ms (set up in the configuration)

 A 10 MB file download over HTTP/3

 Using CUBIC congestion control

 With 30% random packet loss injected during the first two seconds 

 After two seconds, loss stops entirely

 The test has a generous 10-second timeout to complete the download, which is expected to be completed in four or five seconds

 The expected behavior is straightforward: CUBIC should take some hits during the loss phase, reduce its congestion window, and once loss stops, steadily ramp up and finish the download well within the timeout.

However, the number of bytes in flight remains flat, which contradicts the core logic of the CUBIC algorithm: in the absence of loss, apply more gas to increase throttle (more bytes in our world).
sections.impact:
The simulated test setup includes the following details:

 
 
 Quiche HTTP/3 client and server running at locally (localhost)

 RTT = 10ms (set up in the configuration)

 A 10 MB file download over HTTP/3

 Using CUBIC congestion control

 With 30% random packet loss injected during the first two seconds 

 After two seconds, loss stops entirely

 The test has a generous 10-second timeout to complete the download, which is expected to be completed in four or five seconds

 The expected behavior is straightforward: CUBIC should take some hits during the loss phase, reduce its congestion window, and once loss stops, steadily ramp up and finish the download well within the timeout. When we zoom into that region, our analysis shows that CUBIC enters a rapid oscillation, shown in our plot as an extended recovery phase, between congestion avoidance state (the operational regime phase) and recovery state (the packet loss recovery state) — 999 transitions in approximately 6.7 seconds . Whatever is triggering the recovery/avoidance flip is happening once per round trip, in lockstep with connection's ACK clock; the self-clocking rhythm in which each round-trip's ACKs from the client trigger the server's next send. Because this is a download (server to client), the ACKs in question travel client to server, and CUBIC's state machine runs on the server side: every time those ACKs land, bytes_in_flight drops to zero and the server sends the next two-packet burst, which is what triggers the bug.

Classified impact band: medium. Affected layer signal: edge — cdn.
sections.resolution:
Validation 
 
 
 
 
 With the fix applied, the 100% pass rate of our quiche testing suite was restored.

The story starts with a Linux kernel change aimed at bringing CUBIC into line with the app-limited exclusion described in RFC 9438 §4.2-12 — a fix to a real problem in TCP that, when ported to our QUIC implementation, surfaced unexpected behaviors in quiche.

It has a happy ending: an elegant (near-)one-line fix that broke the cycle.

The results were conclusive: 100% pass rate, showing Reno recovered cleanly after the loss phase, and revealing that this is a CUBIC-related bug.
sections.lessons_learned: This reads as a retrospective or postmortem-style narrative; extract concrete action items from the source.
sections.root_cause_highlight: Likely root-cause theme: network issue. In essence, CCAs aim to maximize data transfer by inferring the "available bandwidth" of the network; because no one wants to pay for a 1 Gbps subscription and only use a fraction of it. Every loss-based CCA, CUBIC included, is ultimately a policy for how to grow cwnd when the network looks healthy and how to shrink it when it doesn't. The family of loss-based algorithms, to which CUBIC belongs, operate on a fundamental premise: (1) if there is no packet loss, increase the sending rate (i.e. increase the bandwidth utilization); (2) if there is loss, loss-based algorithms assume that the network's capacity has been exceeded, and the sender must back off (i.e. decrease the bandwidth utilization).

title: Red Hat Hardened Images: Top 5 benefits for software developers
summary: When you use a standard container image, you usually get a lot of extra software you didn't ask for. This extra software can bring along security bugs because it effectively increases the attack surface. Once bugs are present, you have to spend time looking at them, looking for resolutions, workarounds or just proving they don't matter. This takes time away from writing code.
timeline_entries: 1. Fewer security alerts The primary challenge in security today isn't finding bugs; it is the time spent fixing them. Recent industry publications suggest the biggest bottleneck for teams is remediation toil.
Developer tools: These include tools for development and testing, such as curl and jq (a tool for processing JSON data).
These images are updated frequently to ensure they remain secure and compatible with the latest versions of your favorite languages.

5. Tested by Red Hat Red Hat does more than provide small images; we make sure they work. We track where the code comes from and test it to make sure it is stable. If a new bug is found in the foundation, we provide a fi

sections.overview: When you use a standard container image, you usually get a lot of extra software you didn't ask for. This extra software can bring along security bugs because it effectively increases the attack surface. Once bugs are present, you have to spend time looking at them, looking for resolutions, workarounds or just proving they don't matter.
sections.technical_breakdown:
Databases and data management: These include containerized, SQL-compliant databases like MariaDB and PostgreSQL, as well as key-value stores such as Memcached and Valkey.

The image uses approximately 10% of the space on your system and moves across the network faster during deployment.

Networking: These include tools like dig and Unbound for troubleshooting DNS issues.

When you use a standard container image, you usually get a lot of extra software you didn't ask for.

This week Red Hat announced Red Hat Hardened Images , small containers that incorporate a minimal set of files for delivering purpose-built applications .
sections.impact: Classified impact band: medium. Affected layer signal: data store.
sections.resolution:
Once bugs are present, you have to spend time looking at them, looking for resolutions, workarounds or just proving they don't matter.

Fewer security alerts The primary challenge in security today isn't finding bugs; it is the time spent fixing them.

If a new bug is found in the foundation, we provide a fix quickly.
sections.lessons_learned:
By starting with a cleaner image, we help you remove the tasks that prevent you from focusing on your core work.

Better performance with smaller image footprints Because these images are smaller, they are faster.
sections.root_cause_highlight: Likely root-cause theme: deployment failure. This extra software can bring along security bugs because it effectively increases the attack surface. Industry analysts predict that by 2027, 30% of application vulnerabilities will be caused by vibe coding —code generated using AI without a full understanding of the underlying code. Better performance with smaller image footprints Because these images are smaller, they are faster.

title: How EvalHub manages two-layer Kubernetes control planes
summary: Running AI evaluations in production is not a one-time script. It is a continuous operational discipline. This includes scheduling compute-intensive jobs, managing concurrency, tracking experiments across model versions, enforcing resource quotas of workloads competing for precious accelerator resources, and surviving cluster restarts without losing state.
timeline_entries: Running AI evaluations in production is not a one-time script. It is a continuous operational discipline. This includes scheduling compute-intensive jobs, managing concurrency, tracking experiments across model versions,
A framework-agnostic orchestration layer Red Hat AI 3.4 introduces the evaluation hub, a unified control plane for AI evaluation and safety capabilities based on the upstream EvalHub project.

The core value proposition is simple: We do not force you to choose the evaluation frameworks we "think" are best. Because new evaluation harnesses and safety techniques are published almost every week, the evaluation hu
Onboard any framework: Move to the latest techniques the moment they are released.

The second layer is the EvalHub Server . It acts as the evaluation orchestration control plane. It exposes a versioned REST API ( /api/v1/... ) and receives evaluation requests from clients such as code, notebooks, CI pi

Inside the EvalHub server Once the operator brings the EvalHub pod online, the server handles the runtime orchestration. The server is written in Go. It uses the standard net/http router, structured JSON logging with zap

The storage layer is abstracted behind a pluggable interface. It supports SQLite in-memory for local development and single-node testing and PostgreSQL for production multi-replica deployments. Configuration is loaded fr

sections.overview: Running AI evaluations in production is not a one-time script. It is a continuous operational discipline. This includes scheduling compute-intensive jobs, managing concurrency, tracking experiments across model versions, enforcing resource quotas of workloads competing for precious accelerator resources, and surviving cluster restarts without losing state.
sections.technical_breakdown:
The EvalHub custom resource You can deploy EvalHub to a Kubernetes or Red Hat OpenShift cluster using a single YAML declaration managed by the TrustyAI Service Operator:

 apiVersion: trustyai.opendatahub.io/v1alpha1
kind: EvalHub
metadata:
 name: evalhub
 namespace: my-namespace
spec:
 replicas: 1
 env:
 - name: MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI
 value: "http://mlflow:5000" The operator watches for these resources and reconciles them into a running deployment.

It uses the standard net/http router, structured JSON logging with zap , and Prometheus metrics.

It supports SQLite in-memory for local development and single-node testing and PostgreSQL for production multi-replica deployments.

The provider YAML declares this deployment model:

 id: lighteval
runtime:
 k8s:
 image: quay.io/evalhub/community-lighteval:latest
 entrypoint: [python, main.py]
 cpu_request: 100m
 memory_request: 128Mi This is the same pattern any BYOF adapter uses.

GuideLLM measures throughput, latency, memory usage, and token cost across hardware configurations.
sections.impact:
These clients support multi-tenant deployments through a per-request tenant parameter.

 evalhub.cli The evalhub.cli module is a fully implemented CLI ( evalhub ) built on Click. In multi-tenant deployments, experiments are scoped to the tenant's namespace. This ensures every SQL query is automatically filtered by tenant scope without extra logic in every handler. You can register providers as system -scoped or tenant -scoped.

Classified impact band: high. Affected layer signal: platform — kubernetes.
sections.resolution: Resolution detail may be partial in the RSS excerpt. Refer to the original write-up for timelines, mitigations, and verification steps.
sections.lessons_learned: Lessons are not explicit in the excerpt; use the source discussion for durable engineering takeaways.
sections.root_cause_highlight: Likely root-cause theme: control plane issue. A framework-agnostic orchestration layer Red Hat AI 3.4 introduces the evaluation hub, a unified control plane for AI evaluation and safety capabilities based on the upstream EvalHub project. Because new evaluation harnesses and safety techniques are published almost every week, the evaluation hub acts as a framework-agnostic orchestration layer. Evaluations are not run on Kubernetes as an afterthought; the cluster is the control plane.

title: Connecting the dots for accurate AI
summary: At HumanX, Ryan is joined by Philip Rathle, CTO at Neo4j to discuss what knowledge context means for AI agents, how limitations like stale training data make the model-only approach to agents a bad fit for enterprise environments, and how Graph RAG raises the bar for accuracy and reduces context rot by combining vectors with a knowledge graph so agents are more targeted and connected.
